preamble: Lateralization (or Lambdacism) is the shift of a stop consonant to a lateral liquid. In Italic and Hellenic linguistics, this is a famous historical shift where alveolar [d] became [l].

examples.note: Archaic Latin shift of initial d- to l-.
Variation between stop and lateral in Latin roots.
Borrowing or shared root showing d/l alternation between Greek and Latin.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
